The round perforation on one of the megaliths is a common feature in megalithic","inscription:en:1"=>"structures and can even be seen on most of the Ggantija temples' doorways.","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"One of the most characteristic trees in our landscape is the carob. it is believed that the Arabs introduced the species into the Maltese Islands from870 to 1090 AD. [...]","tourism"=>"information"
"description"=>"commemorating the acceptance of the site as UNESCO World Heritage","historic"=>"memorial"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"The evergreen Aleppo Pine tree is a Maltese indigenous tree and grows in most Mediterranean countries. Remains of pine trees have been found in Quaternary (Ice Age) deposits but these have been eradicated from the wild in Malta at the beginning of the 20t","mapillary"=>"1062364728331634","operator"=>"Heritage Malta","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"Amongst the trees that characterise the Maltese landscape, the Sandarac gum tree is one of the rarest. [...]","mapillary"=>"955132166206629","operator"=>"Heritage Malta","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"Circular holes, cutting through the stone, are visible in some of the large floor slabs, often close to doorways. It is not clear what these holes were used for. The pouring of liquid offerings has often been suggested but it has not yet been proven.","tourism"=>"information"

"board_type"=>"history","information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"The main entrance to Ggantija South is marked by a large threshold slab. Excavations carried out in 1933 found, hidden beneath this slab, some pottery sherds, a bowl filled with some 158 seashells and a horn core of a bull. The latter can be seen at the","inscription:en:1"=>"Ggantija Interpretation Centre. To the left of the main doorway, stone spheres of different sizes can be seen. It is believed that these stones could have been utilised in the construction of the building.","mapillary"=>"355154054006926","operator"=>"Heritage Malta","tourism"=>"information"

"board_type"=>"history","information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"On each side of the doorway leading into the temple, circular hollows are carved opposite each other on two large megaliths.[...]","mapillary"=>"2773704926115576","operator"=>"Heritage Malta","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"In front of the megalithic façade, a large forecourt rests on a man-made terrace supported by a wall of very large blocks of stone which survives in part. Archaeological excavations of the terrace uncovered pottery dating to the late Neolithic period.","inscription:en:1"=>"on the other side of the valley stands Nuffara hill, where evidence for a Bronze Age community was discovered dating around 1500-700BC.","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"The doorway to the temple is built in the shape known as trilithion, in which two large standing stones on each side hold up a large horizontal slab. None of the horizontal slabs (lintels) survive at Ggantija although the notches which held it in place","inscription:en:1"=>"are still visible on this doorway.","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"The Maltese rubble wall is a common characteristic of the local rural scene. Its strength and durability comes from the skilful placing of stones so that each one is locked securely into place. These walls are built by experts in this type of masonry work","mapillary"=>"914316413720942","operator"=>"Heritage Malta","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"From the megalithic facade, only the outer corners remain standing. The central part situated between the two main doorways, was already in ruins by 1827 when it was painted by Charles Frederick de Brocktorff. It is possible that this collapse happened","inscription:en:1"=>"during, or soon after, the period when the building was in use. Metal scaffolding was installed in front of the facade and inside Ggantija South in the 1990s to strengthen the walls and protect them from collapse. Heritage Malta is currently conducting","inscription:en:2"=>"scientific studies to identify better ways of consolidating the megalithic structure.","mapillary"=>"759862862334127","operator"=>"Heritage Malta","tourism"=>"information"
"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"The arrangement of stones at the back [...]","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"A remarkable space, this richly furnished room was very popular with 19th-century artists. Thanks to their paintings, particularly those of Charles Frederick de Brocktorff (1775-1850), we know that several features, such as stone blocks decorated with","inscription:en:1"=>"carved spiral motifs once decorated this apse.","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"Despite the poor soil cover and generally arid environment, the Maltese countryside harbours a surprising range of habitat types which add variety to the landscape. [...]","mapillary"=>"353904794273641","operator"=>"Heritage Malta","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"The North Cave was discovered in 1949. Originally, it appears to have been a rock-cut tomb but from the number of pottery sherds and fragments of animal bone that were discovered inside it, it seems that by the late Neolithic, it might have served as a ru","inscription:en:1"=>"bbish pit.","tourism"=>"information"

"information"=>"board","inscription:en"=>"The typical habitat on the Ggantija plateau is the garrigue, a seemingly arid and treeless environment. In reality, this is one of the richest habitats on the Maltese islands with hundreds of flowering plants, including Giant Fennel, the Wild Thyme, the M","mapillary"=>"1476702236259647","operator"=>"Heritage Malta","tourism"=>"information"
